_meta.purpose: Ground-truth query -> relevant-chunk labels for the RAG eval (design §9). Consumed by scripts/eval-rag.mjs.
_meta.corpus: test/fixtures/rag/eval-corpus/{docs,src} (folder ingest), test/fixtures/rag/site/*.html (website extractor, offline), plus a temp git repo the eval builds with pinned content. Ingested under codenames eval-docs, eval-src, eval-site, eval-repo.
labeling_method: Labels were hand-marked by READING the corpus and identifying which chunk(s) actually contain the answer to a hand-written question — NOT by running any ranker and recording its output. This keeps the gold non-circular: it is derived from content, never from a system-under-test.

Each label is a CONTENT ANCHOR: a distinctive substring that must appear in exactly one chunk. The eval matches anchors against a whitespace-normalized copy of each chunk's text (collapsing newlines/indent), so an anchor survives source line-wrapping AND is resilient to re-chunking as long as the phrase stays within one chunk. The eval asserts every anchor resolves to EXACTLY ONE active chunk; a stale anchor fails the run loudly.

'relevant' lists every chunk that genuinely answers the query (often more than one, across sources) — hit@k counts a hit if ANY of them lands in the top k. This is deliberately lenient toward correct-but-alternative sources; it never inflates a single ranker.

'span' marks the contiguous run of chunks the neighbor-expansion rule should reconstruct for that query (first..last within ONE source). Single-chunk answers have first==last. Only Q5 spans two adjacent chunks (Damage Types + Armor Tiers) — used to measure expansion recall across a real section boundary.

Queries use natural question vocabulary a user would type; the terms naturally co-occur with the answer chunk. That is normal for retrieval eval and is not circular — circularity would be picking the label from a ranker's result, which was not done.
_meta.frozen_against: Chunker defaults MIN=100/TARGET=400/MAX=600 (src/rag/chunker.mjs CHUNK_DEFAULTS). Changing those defaults can move chunk boundaries and requires re-checking anchors (the eval's exactly-one-chunk assertion catches breakage).
_meta.discriminators: Q17 and Q18 are deliberate ranking discriminators: their answer lives ONLY in prose, yet a repetitive long code chunk (quality.mjs / harvest.mjs) has a higher RAW query-term count. A term-count baseline (no IDF, no length normalization) ranks the code chunk first and misses hit@1; both BM25 rankers rank the prose first. They give the eval the power to separate the variants — without them every query saturates.
_meta.honesty: n is small (18) — treat every metric as INDICATIVE, not conclusive. A single query moving is ~5.6 percentage points.

queries.query: how does the yield modifier scale with node richness
relevant: yield modifier stacks additively from the tool tier and multiplicatively from the node richness
The yield modifier multiplies base resource output

span.first: yield modifier stacks additively from the tool tier and multiplicatively from the node richness
span.last: yield modifier stacks additively from the tool tier and multiplicatively from the node richness

queries.rationale: The gathering guide's Yield Modifier section states the additive/multiplicative stacking rule; the site's Yield details page states the same relationship. Both are correct answers.

queries.query: what are the five resource quality tiers named
relevant: crude, common, fine, pristine, and flawless
QUALITY_TIERS lists the five named tiers

span.first: crude, common, fine, pristine, and flawless
span.last: crude, common, fine, pristine, and flawless

queries.rationale: The Resource Quality Tiers section enumerates the five tiers in prose; quality.mjs encodes the same list in code. IDF test: 'pristine'/'flawless' are rare discriminating terms.

queries.query: why do crafters pay a premium for high quality stock
relevant: crafters pay a steep premium for pristine and flawless

span.first: crafters pay a steep premium for pristine and flawless
span.last: crafters pay a steep premium for pristine and flawless

queries.rationale: The Market Premium chunk is the only one that explains the premium mechanism and the gatherer-crafter loop it closes.

queries.query: parry timing window before a guard break
relevant: the parry window opens for a few frames

span.first: the parry window opens for a few frames
span.last: the parry window opens for a few frames

queries.rationale: Parry And Guard Break is the only chunk defining the parry window and the riposte.

queries.query: which damage type does heavy plate armor resist and which defeats it
relevant: Attacks deal one of three damage types: slash, pierce, and crush
heavy armor is rigid plate that stops slash and pierce cold but transmits crush

span.first: Attacks deal one of three damage types: slash, pierce, and crush
span.last: heavy armor is rigid plate that stops slash and pierce cold but transmits crush

queries.rationale: The answer legitimately spans two consecutive sections: Damage Types defines the three types, Armor Tiers states heavy plate stops slash+pierce but takes crush. Multi-chunk span for expansion recall across a section boundary.

queries.query: dodge roll invincibility frames beat an unblockable grab
relevant: The dodge roll grants invincibility frames

span.first: The dodge roll grants invincibility frames
span.last: The dodge roll grants invincibility frames

queries.rationale: Dodge And Invincibility Frames is the only chunk describing i-frames beating an unblockable grab.

queries.query: what happens when poise breaks during a stagger
relevant: once poise breaks the victim is locked in a stagger animation

span.first: once poise breaks the victim is locked in a stagger animation
span.last: once poise breaks the victim is locked in a stagger animation

queries.rationale: Stamina And Poise is the only chunk defining the stagger-on-poise-break rule.

queries.query: how does input resource quality propagate into the crafted output
relevant: the resource quality of the inputs carries forward into the crafted output

span.first: the resource quality of the inputs carries forward into the crafted output
span.last: the resource quality of the inputs carries forward into the crafted output

queries.rationale: Quality Propagation is the defining chunk for the input->output quality rule.

queries.query: does the player character level change gathering yield
relevant: Yield never depends on the player character level directly

span.first: Yield never depends on the player character level directly
span.last: Yield never depends on the player character level directly

queries.rationale: Vocabulary/negation test: the Yield Modifier chunk explicitly answers (yield does NOT depend on level). Same chunk as Q1, different query intent — flagged as a likely hard case.

span.last: clampYield constrains a computed yield to the sane

queries.query: refinery pipeline converts ore into ingots
relevant: The refinery pipeline converts raw ore into refined ingots
The refinement pipeline runs three ordered stages

span.first: The refinery pipeline converts raw ore into refined ingots
span.last: The refinery pipeline converts raw ore into refined ingots

queries.query: what did the second commit add to the refinery
relevant: Added the dedicated smelting stage module and this changelog
add smelting stage module and changelog

span.first: Added the dedicated smelting stage module and this changelog
span.last: Added the dedicated smelting stage module and this changelog

queries.rationale: Git ingest with include_log: the CHANGELOG chunk and the synthetic _commits doc both record the second commit's additions.

queries.query: what does resource quality mean when rolling a harvest
relevant: Resource quality is rolled per harvest across five named tiers

span.first: Resource quality is rolled per harvest across five named tiers
span.last: Resource quality is rolled per harvest across five named tiers

queries.rationale: DISCRIMINATOR. The prose (Resource Quality Tiers) explains the per-harvest roll against the node's quality band. quality.mjs merely lists the tier names but repeats 'quality'/'tiers'/'resource' enough that a term-count baseline ranks it #1 and misses the prose at hit@1; both BM25 rankers (IDF + length-normalized) rank the prose #1. The code chunk is NOT the meaning-answer, so it is deliberately not marked relevant.

queries.query: a tier-three harvester on a rich node returns more ore per swing
relevant: a tier-three harvester on a rich node returns far more ore per swing

span.first: a tier-three harvester on a rich node returns far more ore per swing
span.last: a tier-three harvester on a rich node returns far more ore per swing

queries.rationale: DISCRIMINATOR. The exact scaling claim is stated in the Yield Modifier prose. harvest.mjs computes yield from tier and richness and repeats those tokens, so a term-count baseline ranks the code chunk #1 and misses the prose at hit@1; both BM25 rankers rank the prose #1. The code is not the stated-claim answer and is not marked relevant.
